I see that the controls have established the baseline, and then relative to the baseline, the right hemisphere patients, as before, show an impairment on the melody task.
Now we get the opposite pattern of results with the left hemisphere patients.
They show a selective impairment on the rhythm task. And if we got this pattern of results here, this is what we'd call a double dissociation.
Before we had the single dissociation, one patient group selectively impaired on one task, but now we get the double dissociation.
That basically means that we have one patient group that's impaired on one task, the right hemisphere patients on the melody, and the opposite problem for the left hemisphere patients.

And this is a really powerful result for drawing strong inferences because this pattern of results rules out ideas like one task is harder than the other because, again, one group's having trouble with one task, a different group with the other task.
And so if we have this pattern of results, we can make very strong inferences.
For one thing, we could say that it really seems that rhythm and melody are two separate operations because you can have an impairment on one and not on the other.
It also says that, again, probably the right hemisphere is associated with melody, and, surprisingly, the left hemisphere is associated with rhythm.

And it also actually allows us to analyze our two models because it rules out the serial model because the serial model says that, hey, if I have a problem on rhythm, then I have to have a problem on melody because first I have to do rhythm to get to melody.
And so that double dissociation would say that this is sort of the way to think about music perception, that there are actually two very different contributions of the two hemispheres.
They're both operating in maybe a parallel manner. And our percept of music is some sort of integrated percept that emerges from those two different operations.

This might sound like a bit of a hokey example, but there's actually data that look just like this about people looking at the neuropsychology of music perception and seeing these fundamental differences between the two different hemispheres and how they contribute to the overall percept.
Okay, so, again, one thing to keep in mind that these neuropsychological studies are in a sense correlational.
We're looking at a correlation between brain injury and changes in behavior.
So there's always limitations whenever we're doing correlational studies, but there's also other sorts of limitations with the methods.
But it is one way that people have studied about how do you look at a perturbed brain.
Usually in these studies, we're looking at impairments, right, we're looking at deficits in performance following brain injury.
Occasionally, you get these very bizarre cases where it looks like brain injury in some way has helped individuals.

Here's just one example, okay, where they took a bunch of people, military vets from Vietnam, okay, they had high combat exposure, and they wanted to basically say we could look at their brain scans, okay, and we could look to see where there was damage.

And they're basically color-coding things here in terms of the degree of damage that they can identify structurally in the brains of these vets who have, and basically, they're color-coding the extent of the damage.
So the brighter the color, the more damage. The bluer the color, the less damage.

And they basically then said, you know, is there a correlation, because it's a correlational method between where the damage is and the likelihood, the incidence that the vet's gonna be experiencing PTSD, right, some sort of traumatic or psychiatric condition related frequently to combat experience.
And the interesting result here is that actually having damage in the amygdala actually was associated with, it almost was like a safety measure from getting PTSD.
It was like a protection from getting PTSD was to have had damage in this one part of the brain.
And here they have other types of conditions here. The ventromedial prefrontal cortex also had a fairly low rate, so they also had some protection.
So basically, having damage in this sort of, these regions of the brain down here seem to provide some protective value from developing PTSD.
Now, presumably, there's some cost also to having damage to those parts of the brain.
But if you remember, I showed back that optogenetic stimulation, that if you stimulated the amygdala, the rats became less fearful, and it seems to be something about, again, this.
change in the amygdala is actually reducing the amount of fear and the over-responsiveness that individuals have with PTSD.
Now, of course, with lesions, the things are always messy.

These are naturally occurring lesions, so you're never gonna get identical overlap between your group of individuals, and that's why we can use crude terms, or use methods to look at the degree of overlap.

Many cases, we're studying people who've had a stroke, say, five or 10 years ago, and the brain might have become reorganized, and so we don't know if we're actually making inferences about what that part of the brain that's damaged usually does or whether we're looking at a condition where the brain is compensated for the injury.
But again, it's all correlational. And so there's a number of different experimental methods where people think about how we can manipulate the brain.

We've talked about a few of these already. There's a whole world of pharmacology where basically we know, we've talked about that, there's different neurotransmitters allowing neurons to communicate with one another.
Well, there's all sorts of pharmacological manipulations that we can boost up the level of dopamine or reduce the level of dopamine, one transmitter versus another.
Basically, that's the whole field of psychiatry. In a sense, that's an ongoing experiment, right?

That is a science of trying to see how do we manipulate the balance between different types of neurotransmitters and what kind of effect will that have on a person's behavior, presumably by altering their brain function.

And at Berkeley, there's a whole center now for psychedelic science, which is also basically looking at pharmacological manipulations and asking how does this change or how does taking psilocybin, how does that affect your perception, your consciousness, and so forth?

So those are experimental methods, right? In those studies, usually giving one drug versus a placebo or giving one dose versus a high, low dose versus a high dose, the experiment is manipulating the situation.
We also have talked briefly about invasive brain stimulation, neural stimulation.

That would be an experimental procedure. Again, I gave you that optogenetics example where we're experimentally turning on and off neurons in a particular part of the brain and asking how does that change behavior?
So that's an experimental manipulation. There's also various people out there doing different types of direct cortical stimulation in humans.
Some of it's usually done as part of surgical procedures.
Some of it's done as interventions. We'll talk about it later in the semester about deep brain stimulation as a treatment for, say, Parkinson's disease.
But today, I really wanna focus on what we call noninvasive brain stimulation.

And this is a whole class of methods that have been developed to use safely in humans as a way, usually these developed techniques are developed with some clinical purpose in mind, but experimentalists have come along and said these can be a really interesting tool to actually look about what happens, how can we alter behavior or study changes in behavior as we perturb targeted regions of the brain.
And again, it's done as a safe procedure. Lots of studies going to establish that these are safe methods.
And they're transient. They're gonna do very brief perturbations of the brain.

That's one of the signatures that the muscle is being activated.
Then there's this silent period right afterwards. You make a bunch of that neurons fire and then they have this refractory period.
The timing of this is very precise. It's about 17 milliseconds to get from the primary motor cortex, single synapse, down to the spinal cord, and then out to the arm to activate the muscle.
Disynaptic synapses there are going to get you about 17 milliseconds, or there might be a third synapse, spinal interneuron.

Pre-synapse is basically from the brain to the hand and I can now test people who have neurological disorders, like something like multiple sclerosis, and I'd see that maybe that timing there has been disrupted and so forth.

Again, this is one of the clinical purposes that now we have a way to measure the integrity of the motor pathways because we can see are these motor revoked potentials of normal amplitude and especially is the timing intact.
What's really caught on though is that actually TMS is being used for many different clinical disorders and most notably is in the treatment of depression.
The protocol they use currently, it's changing all the time, but this is one of the types of protocols they'll do.
They'll give you 40 pulses for four seconds, so at a very fast rate, one every 100 milliseconds, they'll give you a pulse.
It's usually over the prefrontal cortex. Then there'll be a pause and then they'll repeat this over and over again.

Patients who come in for the standard treatment come in four or five times a week for three weeks, so they're going to get about 40,000 of these pulses over the course of the treatment period there, but this has been approved by the FDA.
Now the FDA approves things basically if they do no harm and if they do at least as good as placebo.
Okay, they don't have a really high bar in efficacy.
There has to be some indication there, but it's not a very strong one, okay?
It's got to be some indication there, but again, it's caught on and there's mixed results.
There seems to be some benefit there, can be lasting in some individuals, but certainly not a panacea.

It hasn't been a breakthrough treatment in depression, but on the other hand, depression has been a really tricky thing for pharmacology and the people who get TMS the most always had to go through three rounds of different types of drug treatment that has failed to work for the individuals, so you're really getting the drug-resistant individuals who are actually getting tested to see if TMS treats them there.
This is just a video showing you what it's like when you get that high-frequency stimulation.

cranked up there because it was a single pulse so that he could, you know, ensure that he got an effect, yeah.

I was demonstrating over motor cortex, but I could TMS over different parts of the brain and ask what changes in behavior occur if I transiently, very briefly, perturb the neural activity in the tissue that's underlying the TMS coil.
And so here's just one early demonstration that's from your textbook.
It's basically showing here's, what they're going to do is they're going to flash this letter on the screen, it's going to be flashed for a very short amount of time.
Our visual system is really good at picking up brief flashes of information.
The person's task is simply say, what's the letter you saw there?
Okay, so if it was that trial, you'd say, K.
I could flash it for almost as fast as the computer monitor could go, and you'd do great at that task.
Okay? But what they wanted to ask here is, suppose we TMS over the visual areas of the brain.
So back here over the occipital lobe, is there a particular time window between when the letter flashes, at which point I'll actually disrupt their ability to identify the letter?
So zero here, right here, so notice if I flash the letter well before I do the TMS pulse, this is the time between the flash and the TMS pulse.
So zero means the TMS pulse comes right when the letter appears, 100 means the TMS pulse comes 100 milliseconds after the letter was flashed.
If we do it well before, if we do the TMS pulse well before the stimulus occurs, you get 100% correct.
If I actually do it right at the time of the flash, flash the letter, hit the occipital lobe, they're also perfect, okay?

But if I actually wait 80, 100, 120 milliseconds, now suddenly if I perturb the visual cortex, the person's performance drops dramatically, in fact, they're almost like in a sense functionally blind if I actually TMS them 100 milliseconds after I flash the letter.

So that's told me a lot, it says first of all visual cortex is indeed essential for this perception, but it also says that the critical time point when the occipital lobe is important is actually about a tenth of a second after the stimulus appears, and presumably that's because the information has to be processed through the eyes, relayed up to the thalamus, and then get up into the cortex for it to do its work, and it's probably doing its work about 100 milliseconds after the flash occurs.

And the idea here is sort of captured in this cartoon here, is that presumably we have some pattern of neural activity that allows us to recognize, say, the letter A if that's what was flashed, but when I apply that TMS pulse, some neurons are being activated by the stimulus, but a bunch of the other neurons are being activated by the TMS pulse, and now the brain has this difficult what we call signal-to-noise problem, how does it pull out the information from the neurons that are being driven by the stimulus, how does it separate that from all those neurons that are being activated by the TMS pulse, and so you get this dramatic drop off in performance, but if you wait longer and do the TMS pulse, the occipital lobe is quickly recovered.

So that's what I mean by it's a very transient perturbation, but now I have the safe way that I can basically disrupt one part of the brain and ask how it changes behavior, or I could do it for any part of the brain.

All right, skip that. Well, here's a really cool study taking actually a combination of brain imaging, which we're going to talk about next week, and TMS to ask a really, I think, a really cool question, okay?

So, what had happened is, you know, very basic question came along when people had tools to look at brain activity, is they took individuals who were blind, and they asked, you know, what happens to their occipital lobe?
People who are blind from birth, right, they have the parts of the brain that are typically associated with vision, aren't receiving input because they've been blind from birth, okay?
And they said, what happens when they're reading Braille? Okay?
So now, again, now they're actually sensing the world tactilely, right?
And what they found in the imaging studies is that studies is that the blind individuals were showing pronounced activation in the occipital lobe when they were reading with Braille.
They were also, of course, showing activation in the primary somatosensory cortex, so the part of the tissue where we know the inputs are coming from the fingers.
